title: Treacle tart
subtitle: Golden syrup and breadcrumb tart in a shortcrust shell.
excerpt: A buttery shortcrust pastry case filled with golden syrup, breadcrumbs and lemon juice, baked until the filling is set and faintly caramelised at the edges. One of the great British puddings. Serves six to eight.
sourceNotes: Treacle tart. The combination of golden syrup and breadcrumbs in a pastry case is documented in British cookbooks from the late 19th century, shortly after Lyle's Golden Syrup was first manufactured in 1883. Mrs Beeton's later editions and late Victorian domestic manuals contain versions of the recipe. The tart has been a fixture of British school dinners and home baking ever since.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked tart for up to two months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the pastry the day before and refrigerate. The baked tart keeps for three days at room temperature.

content.text: Treacle tart is simpler than it sounds: a pastry case with a golden syrup filling thickened with breadcrumbs and sharpened with lemon. The breadcrumbs absorb the syrup as the tart bakes; too few and the filling is runny, too many and it is stodgy. 75 g to 350 g syrup is the correct ratio. Serve warm with cream or cold the next day.
Make and blind bake the pastry

Rub {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter into {{plain-flour}} of flour and {{icing-sugar}} of icing sugar until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Add {{eggs}} egg yolk and {{water}} of ice-cold water; mix until the dough just comes together. Flatten into a disc, wrap and chill for 30 minutes. Roll out to 3 mm thick and line a 23 cm tart tin. Chill for another 15 minutes. Blind bake at 180°C for 15 minutes with baking paper and weights, then remove the paper and bake for 5 minutes more until lightly golden.

Warm {{golden-syrup}} of golden syrup gently until runny. Stir in {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Pour into the pastry case. Bake at 180°C for 20 to 25 minutes until the filling is set with a slight wobble in the centre. Cool for at least 20 minutes before slicing; the filling firms as it cools.

items.fix: Bake until the edges are set and the centre has only a very faint wobble. The filling continues to set as it cools.
The base needs 15 minutes fully blind baked, then 5 minutes uncovered before the filling goes in. This seals the base against the syrup.

items.symptom: Filling too sweet with no contrast
items.fix: Squeeze the full juice of a lemon. The acid is essential to balance the sweetness of 350 g of golden syrup.

content.text: Treacle tart is the sweet end of the British school dinner tradition: simple, filling, and inseparable from a specific kind of childhood comfort. Outside Britain it is virtually unknown, though it requires nothing exotic. The only ingredient peculiar to British kitchens is golden syrup, which can be ordered online if it is not on the shelf. Serve warm with cold double cream.
